elements.sent-2411-001: Lossy compression methods rely on an autoencoder to transform a point cloud into latent points for storage, leaving the inherent redundancy of latent representations unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is accomplished by a dual-density scheme separately processing the latent points (intended for reconstruction) and the decoupled sparse priors (intended for storage).
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach features an efficient dual-density data flow that relaxes size constraints on latent points, and hybridizes a progressive conditional diffusion model to encapsulate essential details for reconstruction within the conditions, which are decoupled hierarchically to intra-point and inter-point priors.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, our method encodes the original point cloud into latent points and decoupled sparse priors through separate encoders.
elements.sent-2411-005: Latent points serve as intermediates, while sparse priors act as adaptive conditions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then employ a progressive attention-based conditional denoiser to generate latent points conditioned on the decoupled priors, allowing the denoiser to dynamically attend to geometric and semantic cues from the priors at each encoding and decoding layer.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we integrate the local distribution into the arithmetic encoder and decoder to enhance local context modeling of the sparse points.
elements.sent-2411-008: The original point cloud is reconstructed through a point decoder.
elements.sent-2411-009: Compared to state-of-the-art, our method obtains superior rate-distortion trade-off, evidenced by extensive evaluations on the ShapeNet dataset and standard test datasets from MPEG group including 8iVFB, and Owlii.
elements.sent-2411-010: Point clouds are the most common raw form of 3D data representation and are widely used for representing objects or scenes in various applications such as mixed reality~\cite{sandstrom2023point-mixR , autonomous driving~\cite{yang2024visual-autodrive , and object reconstruction~\cite{wu2023sketch .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: With the rapid advancement of 3D acquisition technology, complex geometries can now be captured in real time as large point clouds that offer detailed geometric information.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, this poses considerable challenges for storing and transmitting such large volumes of data, \textcolor{black {highlighting the need for more efficient point cloud compression techniques that minimize storage footprint while maintaining geometric fidelity.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{quickrun.png} \caption{\label{fig:quickrun} (a) Conventional compressors code the latent representation of points directly via a naive context-free entropy model. (b) \textcolor{black}{Our method codes the sparse priors via a context-aware entropy model. It employs a two-stage data flow to compress the points further into decoupled sparse priors, and incorporates a progressive attention-based conditional diffusion model to denoise the latent representations conditioned on the sparse priors.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Lossy geometry compression methods can be divided into voxel-based and point-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-014: Both employ an encoder to map a point cloud or voxels into low-dimensional latent points as well as attached features, which we call latents briefly unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-015: The latents are then coded into a binary string using an entropy model for storage, and the decoder reconstructs the original point cloud from the decoded latents.
elements.sent-2411-016: Voxel-based methods \cite{wang2021lossy, nguyenLearningBasedLosslessCompression2021 transform point clouds into voxels before applying established image compressors to acquire latent codes.}
elements.sent-2411-017: These methods take advantage of extensively validated image compression techniques but suffer from heavy computational overhead and geometry loss due to voxelization.
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite attempts \citet{wang2021multiscale to reduce memory by sparse convolution \cite{choy20194d , these networks struggle with high sparsity, leading to inadequate local context.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Point-based methods typically utilize an auto-encoder structure, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:quickrun (a).}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the decompressed point clouds tend to lose local details making it difficult to balance the compression rate and reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recently, \citet{he2022density proposed a density-aware encoder to retain local density by enforcing density loss.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, this method suffers from rapid degradation of neighboring information as the compression ratio increases, limiting its effectiveness to scenarios with lower compression ratios.
elements.sent-2411-023: This model introduces a dual-density data flow to remove the redundancy within the latents, leveraging a compact hierarchical representation to maximize the compression of the overall geometry and higher-fidelity latents to faithfully reconstruct the original structure.
elements.sent-2411-024: The sparse points, which are downsampled at a higher rate, act as a more compact version of the latent representation, capturing the overall structure while summarizing the collapsed points that surround the sparse points and are discarded during downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-025: The sparse priors encode the relationships within the collapsed set around each sparse latent point, though it leaves the spatial dependencies between the sparse latent points themselves unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address this limitation, we model each sparse latent point as a composite of local factors, including underlying surfaces, curvature, and point density.
elements.sent-2411-027: A lightweight predictor is then used to learn an adaptive local distribution based on the geometry and context of neighboring points, enabling a more efficient representation of intra-point relationships over a broader receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-028: The PACD effectively captures the inherent variability and uncertainty in the latent space, ensuring that a rich and diverse distribution of both geometric and semantic information is fed to the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-029: Simultaneously, it maintains the completeness and accuracy of the original point cloud geometry while minimizing storage costs, as the diffuser is guided by homogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-030: This separation enables more flexible size constraints on the latents, while ensuring high reconstruction fidelity, even under high compression ratios.
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, to better exploit the intra-point priors, we integrate the local distribution into a context-aware entropy model, thereby improving the binary encoding of sparse points.
elements.sent-2411-032: This approach contrasts with the conventional context-free entropy model, which assumes a global distribution.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-034: By capitalizing on the form simplicity of sparse priors, we relax the size constraints imposed on latents for reconstruction and reduce the inherent redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-035: We introduce a multi-stage sparsity guided conditional diffusion scheme.
elements.sent-2411-036: We decouple the sparse priors into inter-point sparse latents and intra-point local distributions, and employ progressive cross-attention to aggregate priors from both scales across varying down-sampling rates.
elements.sent-2411-037: The sparse mapping is learned jointly with the denoising process to preserve consistent topological and semantic properties with the original point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-038: We present a decoupled sparse priors guided diffusion compression model (DSP-DCM) for point cloud, driven by conditional diffusion model and context-aware entropy model.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our framework denoises the latent representations conditioned on the sparse priors, enabling the generation of high-quality distribution-enhanced structures even at extremely low bitrates.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our inter-point local distribution is also integrated into both the arithmetic encoder and decoder to enhance the contextual modeling of sparse points.
elements.sent-2411-041: Experiments on the ShapeNet and standard MPEG test datasets (8iVFB, Owlii) validate that our method achieves a state-of-the-art rate-distortion performance.
elements.sent-2411-042: Voxel-based methods \cite{nguyenLearningBasedLosslessCompression2021, nguyenMultiscaleDeepContext2021 first voxelize and partition the input point cloud and then feed the cubes to a 2D convolution neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-043: To reconstruct the original point cloud, the topologic relationship between the cubes is also stored with negligible bits.
elements.sent-2411-044: VoxelDNN \cite{nguyenLearningBasedLosslessCompression2021 uses a ResNet backbone to predict the occupancy probability distribution of voxels, which suffers from low encoding and decoding speeds as both processes work in a sequential fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-045: To alleviate this problem, MSVoxelDNN \cite{nguyenMultiscaleDeepContext2021 proposes to predict the distribution in a coarse-to-fine order.}
elements.sent-2411-046: For lossy point cloud compression, \cite{quachLearningConvolutionalTransforms2019, wangLearnedPointCloud2021 treats the mapping from latent representations to original voxels as a binary classification problem.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Wang \cite{wang2021lossy proposed to predict the mean and variance of latent representations to enhance the coding contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Voxel-based methods struggle with imbalanced distributions of voxel occupancy in sparse point clouds and incur high overhead with dense point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-049: In contrast, our method compresses point sets directly, enhancing scalability for dense point clouds while reducing sensitivity to sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-050: Octree-based methods transform a point cloud into an octree with predetermined number of layers, and then an MLP-based network predicts the distribution of non-leaf nodes.
elements.sent-2411-051: Since each non-leaf node has eight children, the compression can be formulated as a classification of 256 categories.
elements.sent-2411-052: OctSqueeze \cite{huangOctSqueezeOctreeStructuredEntropy2020 was the first to introduce learnable octree-based point cloud compression.}
elements.sent-2411-053: It employs multiple MLP layers to predict the occupancy probabilities of child nodes based on information from parent or ancestor nodes.
elements.sent-2411-054: By encoding nodes layer-by-layer, the method allows for simultaneous encoding of multiple nodes within each layer.
elements.sent-2411-055: Similar in concept to OctSqueeze, OctAttention \cite{fuOctAttentionOctreeBasedLargeScale2022 improves node context by incorporating information from nodes within the same layer, achieving high accuracy in probability distribution predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, it experiences slow decompression speeds because non-leaf nodes depend on their siblings despite the use of local window parallelism.
elements.sent-2411-057: EHEM \cite{song2023efficient is proposed as a solution to this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-058: EHEM splits the nodes into two disjoint groups; the first is independent of sibling nodes and the other group just relies on the first group in terms of information from the same layer.
elements.sent-2411-059: This allows for the simultaneous inference of all nodes within a group strategy and greatly shortens the decoding time.
elements.sent-2411-060: Compared to voxel-based methods, octree-based methods are more efficient in terms of runtime and memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, they remain sensitive to point distribution and are less suited for dynamic contexts due to the high maintenance costs associated with hierarchical structures.
elements.sent-2411-062: Point-based methods D-PCC \cite{he2022density directly process the geometry by an auto-encoder \cite{hinton2006reducing .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The encoder reduces the point cloud resolution via down-sampling and encodes it into a compact binary format using a bottleneck entropy model.
elements.sent-2411-064: The decoder then reconstructs the full point cloud from the compressed format.
elements.sent-2411-065: The method's performance degrades at high compression ratios due to substantial loss of neighboring information.
elements.sent-2411-066: To address this, we introduce a decoupled data flow that preserves sparse points while employing latent points for point cloud reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-067: This approach effectively generates high-quality, distribution-enhanced structures, even at extremely low bitrates.
elements.sent-2411-068: The diffusion model was first proposed for image generation \cite{ho2020denoising, song2020denoising and has gradually been applied to various tasks for point cloud analysis in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Wu \cite{wu2023sketch proposes to utilize sketches and texts as conditions to generate object-level colored point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-070: This approach involves fusing sketch and point cloud features through a cross-attention mechanism, with separate diffusion models generating geometry and appearance information.
elements.sent-2411-071: Point-E \cite{nichol2022point employs diffusion models to create three-dimensional shapes from images by fine-tuning GLIDE models with specific language prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The first diffusion model generates dense point cloud conditioned on sparse point cloud while the second diffusion model generates corresponding features from dense point cloud, after feeding a point-wise auto-encoder with both geometry and attribute data to reconstruct the point cloud, surface reconstruction is employed to generate the mesh.
elements.sent-2411-073: In our method, diffusion models capture complex data distributions and reduce noise, effectively mitigating data loss from high compression rates.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mainfigure.png} \caption{Overview of our decoupled sparse priors guided diffusion compression model for point cloud (DSP-DCM). Our approach begins with a point encoder that transforms the input point cloud into latent points and features. Instead of directly encoding these latents, we extract sparser representations obtained through a separate sparse encoder. The DCM further decouples the sparser representation into sparse points and intra-point local distributions. During decompression, we start with Gaussian noise and apply a progressive attention-based conditional denoiser (PACD) on the sparse point cloud to reconstruct the latents. These reconstructed latents are then decoded to produce a high-quality point cloud.} \label{fig:ppl}
elements.sent-2411-074: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ppl , the proposed DSP-DCM separately processes latent representations and sparse priors, augmented by a progressive latent diffusion model guided by the sparse priors.}
elements.sent-2411-075: To effectively compress the original point cloud $X$, we address the inherent redundancy within latent points $X_L$ and attached features $F_L$ by using a dual-density data flow in Sec~\ref{subsec:flow , which consists of two separate encoders: point encoder $\Theta_L$ and sparse encoder $\Theta_S$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: These encoders generate $(X_L, F_L)$ and sparse points $(X_S, F_S)$ ensuring that $\lvert X_S \rvert < \lvert X_L \rvert$, while storing only the latter.
elements.sent-2411-077: For simplicity, we will use \(\mathbf{X _S\) to denote the sparse point cloud \(X_S\) along with associated features \(F_S\).}
elements.sent-2411-078: Likewise, \(\mathbf{X _L\) will refer to the pair \((X_L, F_L)\) in the remainder of the discussion unless otherwise specified.}
elements.sent-2411-079: To complement data loss during downsampling, we also predict the local distribution $(\mu_S,\sigma_S)$ for each sparse point as inter-point priors.
elements.sent-2411-080: For high-fidelity reconstruction during decompression in Sec~\ref{subsec:pacd , we condition the latent denoiser $\mathcal{D}$ progressively on the sparse priors, $\mathbf{X _S$ and $(\mu_S,\sigma_S)$, to preserve low-frequencies and deterministic aspects of the original structure.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Subsequently, the predicted latents $\tilde{\mathbf{X _L$ are fed to a point decoder to reconstruct high-quality point cloud $X$.}}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Decoupled Sparse Prior Guided Data Flow
elements.sent-2411-083: The first stage is to scatter the spatial structure and preserve a compact manifestation of the original point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-084: This stage follows \cite{he2022density but separates the latent (intended for reconstruction) and sparse priors (intended for storage) to relax the size constraints on the latents and reduce the redundancy of the latents.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The second stage aims to get a comprehensive yet more compact sparse priors to latent denoising.
elements.sent-2411-086: Firstly, we sample sparse points and attached features to capture the structure surrounding each sparse latent point.
elements.sent-2411-087: To enable a larger receptive field and complement downsampling loss, we predict local distribution that describes the semantic context details, such as underlying surfaces, curvature, and point density.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this way, sparse priors are decoupled into intra-point and inter-point signals.
elements.sent-2411-089: Sparse coding: Our DSP-DCM begins with a point encoder that maps the input point cloud to latent points $X_{L}$ and features $F_{L}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Despite the abstract transformation of large point clouds into latent space, the lack of distinction in the extracted latents leads to redundancy, resulting in unnecessary storage overhead.
elements.sent-2411-091: To address this problem, we aim to learn a more compact representation of the original point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-092: Rather than directly coding and storing the latents, we store a sparser representation that is obtained via a separate encoder $\Theta_S$, which has a much higher down-sampling rate.
elements.sent-2411-093: The sparse representation captures the skeleton of the 3D shape, representing its global structure and local geometric details.
elements.sent-2411-094: During decompression, we begin with a randomly initialized Gaussian noise and apply our PCAD to reconstruct the latents, with the sparse representations serving as the conditions.
elements.sent-2411-095: The reconstructed latents are then fed into the decoder to generate high-fidelity point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-096: We adapt the auto-encoder architecture from D-PCC \cite{he2022density for our application.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Further details regarding model architecture are provided in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-098: Prior decoupling: Initially, the sparse representations are sampled as sparser point cloud $X_S=\{x_{S,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_S}\}$ with associated features $F_S=\{f_{S,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_S}\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The sparse points are then quantized and shifted to a non-negative point cloud $\hat{X _S=\{\hat{x _{S,i}\in{\mathbb{N ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_S}\}$, which is subsequently coded to bitstring and decoded back by an entropy model $\mathcal{E}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The sparse points summarize the collapsed points surrounding them, which are discarded during downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-101: This encoding captures the relationships within the collapsed set around each sparse latent point, but neglects the spatial dependencies among the sparse latent points themselves, which may degrade reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-102: To get a more compact and comprehensive representation, we derive a local distribution feature explicitly, $(\mathcal{\mu _S,\mathcal{\sigma _S)=\{(\mu_i,\sigma_i)|\mu_i\in{\mathbb{R ,\sigma_i\in{\mathbb{R }$\} for sparse points.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This is achieved by a lightweight distribution predictor $\mathcal{P}$, which consists of stacked convolutional layers (which we will explain later in Sec~\ref{subsec:abl ).}
elements.sent-2411-104: These sparse points and their local distributions are summarized as sparse priors, which are then embedded as conditions of the PACD detailed in Sec~\ref{subsec:pacd .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Finally, the point decoder ${\Omega_L}$ reconstructs the original point cloud from denoised latents $\tilde{\mathcal{X _L$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Given the inevitable information loss during down-sampling, the mapping from sparse points to latent points becomes ill-posed and prone to clustering artifacts in the reconstructed point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-107: To address this, we leverage diffusion models, known for their robust generative capabilities, to encapsulate variability inherent in original point cloud $X$, thereby preserving essential details and mitigating inherent outliers through iterative denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-108: To maintain high fidelity of the latents, our DCM incorporates sparse priors into the PCAD at each encoding and decoding stage throughout the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-109: The reverse process of diffusion is modeled as a Markov chain, given by: \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{L,0},\cdots,\mathbf{X}_{L,T-1}|\mathbf{X}_{L,T})=\prod_{t=1}^{T}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t-1}|\mathbf{X}_{L,t},\mathbf{X}_S).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-110: The stepwise denoising follows a Gaussian distribution: \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t-1}|\mathbf{X}_{L,t},\mathbf{X}_S)=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t-1};\mu_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t},\mathbf{X}_S,t),\sigma_t^2\mathcal{I}),\end{equation} and the mean $\mu_{\theta (\mathbf{X _{L,t ,\mathbf{X _S,t)$ is reparameterized as \begin{equation}\mu_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t},\mathbf{X}_S,t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}(\mathbf{X}_{L,t}-\frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}}\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{X}_{L,t},\mathbf{X}_S,t)).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The noise is encapsulated by the denoising network $\epsilon_\theta$, and $\alpha_t$ and $\beta_t$ are predefined small positive constants with $\alpha_t=1-\beta_t$, and the cumulative product $\bar\alpha_t=\prod_{i=1 ^t{\alpha_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Our PCAD employs a U-Net-like architecture, with $N_L$ layers in both the encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-113: At each layer, the sparse point cloud is delicately fused to offer guidance.
elements.sent-2411-114: Each denoising layer processes an input point cloud $\tilde{\mathbf{X _{L,t ^l$, which is intentionally noised based on a pre-scheduled timestep $t$, where $1\leq{l}\leq{N_L}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Given that sparse points $\mathbf{X _S^l$ and latents $\tilde{\mathbf{X _L^l$ are both proper subsets of the original point cloud and share homogeneous characteristics, we safely concatenate them along the point dimension.}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Note that $\mathbf{X _S^l$ is acquired by aligning the feature dimensions of $\mathbf{X _S$ by an MLP for each layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: After using an MLP layer to extract point-wise features from concatenated inputs $(\hat{X _L^l,X_S^l)$, we rely on an improved set abstraction layer \cite{lyu2021conditional to filter out redundant geometric information while preserving essential geometry and semantic candidates.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This results in a coarse prediction $\tilde{\mathbf{F _L^{l-1}$ for $(l-1)_{th}$ layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: We hypothesize that local distribution within the sparse point captures essential inter-point contextual information, which plays a pivotal role in enhancing the accuracy of our coarse predictions.
elements.sent-2411-120: We design a plug-in distribution predictor in the sparse encoder of DCM to predict the local distribution $(\mu_S, \sigma_S)$ and employ a cross-attention mechanism to integrate it with the coarse latent for scalability and explicitness.
elements.sent-2411-121: Using $\tilde{\mathbf{F _L^{l-1}$ as query, $X_S^{l-1}$ as key and $(\mu^{l-1 ,\sigma^{l-1 )$ as value, we are able to propagate the sparse distribution to latents, yielding a distribution-enhanced output $\tilde{\mathbf{F _L^{l-1}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Through iterative downsampling and fusion, we obtain refined features for the latents.
elements.sent-2411-123: The decoder part of the denoiser follows a similar scheme, with the set abstraction replaced by the feature mapper layer (detailed in supplementary material).

elements.sent-2411-180: We introduced a decoupled sparse priors guided compression framework(DSP-DCM).
elements.sent-2411-181: It addresses the inherent redundancy in the latents by decoupling latent points, used for high-fidelity reconstruction, from sparse priors, used for efficient storage and transmission.
elements.sent-2411-182: By leveraging a progressive attention-based conditional denoiser (PACD), DSP-DCM enhances latent reconstruction with sparse priors.
elements.sent-2411-183: DSP-DCM also integrates a context-aware entropy model for optimal binary coding, which ensures minimal data loss and maintains high geometric and semantic integrity.
elements.sent-2411-184: Evaluations on the ShapeNet dataset and MPEG datasets confirm that DSP-DCM achieves state-of-the-art rate-distortion performance.

elements.sent-2411-188: Lossy compression relies on an autoencoder to transform a point cloud into latent points for storage, leaving the inherent redundancy of latent representations unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-189: To reduce redundancy in latent points, we propose a diffusion-based framework guided by sparse priors that achieves high reconstruction quality, especially at low bitrates.
elements.sent-2411-190: Our approach features an efficient dual-density data flow that relaxes size constraints on latent points.
elements.sent-2411-191: It hybridizes a Probabilistic conditional diffusion model to encapsulate essential details for reconstruction within sparse priors, which are decoupled hierarchically into intra and inter-point priors.
elements.sent-2411-192: Specifically, our DiffCom encodes the original point cloud into latent points and decoupled sparse priors through separate encoders.
elements.sent-2411-193: To dynamically attend to geometric and semantic cues from the priors at each encoding and decoding layer, we employ an attention-guided latent denoiser conditioned on the decoupled priors.
elements.sent-2411-194: Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our approach achieves a superior rate-distortion trade-off, as evidenced by extensive evaluations on the ShapeNet dataset and standard test datasets from the MPEG PCC Group.
elements.sent-2411-195: Point clouds efficiently represent 3D geometry by storing only surface samples, avoiding voxel-like memory waste in sparse scenes~\cite{liu2019point,qiPointNetDeepLearning2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-196: Moreover, the unstructured nature of point clouds, characterized by the absence of fixed connectivity, avoids topological constraints and enables flexible and direct geometric editing~\cite{wang2020cascaded .}
elements.sent-2411-197: With the rapid advancement of 3D acquisition technology, complex geometries can now be captured in real time.
elements.sent-2411-198: However, this poses considerable challenges for storing and transmitting such large volumes of data.
elements.sent-2411-199: For instance, each point cloud in the Owlii dataset ~\cite{owlii typically contains over 2.5 million points and occupies approximately 150MB of storage, highlighting the need for more efficient compression techniques that significantly reduce storage footprint without compromising geometric fidelity.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Lossy geometry employs an encoder to map a point cloud into low-dimensional latent points as well as associated features, which we refer to as latents briefly unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-201: Voxel-based methods~\cite{wang2021lossy, nguyenLearningBasedLosslessCompression2021 transform point clouds into voxels before applying volumetric encoders to acquire latent codes.}
elements.sent-2411-202: While these methods can be easily extended from well-established image compression techniques, they suffer from significant computational overhead and geometric loss due to voxelization.
elements.sent-2411-203: Despite attempts to reduce memory~\cite{wang2021multiscale by using sparse convolution~\cite{choy20194d , these networks struggle with high sparsity, resulting in inadequate local context.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: However, the decompressed point clouds tend to lose local details, making it difficult to balance the compression rate and reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-205: Recently, he~et~al.~\cite{he2022density proposed a density-aware encoder to retain local density by enforcing density loss.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, as the compression ratio increases, this method suffers from rapid degradation of local contextual information, imposing a lower bound on the latent redundancy required to maintain acceptable quality.

elements.sent-2411-207: To address redundancy in latent representations, we propose a dual-density data flow, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:quickrun (b).}
elements.sent-2411-208: The data flow separates the functions of reconstruction and storage into two complementary components: dense latents and sparse priors.
elements.sent-2411-209: These latent representations, generated by PointNet-like encoders, consist of point subsets with associated features.
elements.sent-2411-210: The latent points capture an essential global structure, while their features abstract the local context of surrounding points.
elements.sent-2411-211: Previous work has shown that such representations often include redundant points sampling the same surface regions.
elements.sent-2411-212: To address this issue, we extract a sparser subset of latent points, referred to as sparse points, using a higher sampling rate.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, naive dimensionality reduction inevitably results in contextual information loss and fails to capture spatial dependencies among sparse points.
elements.sent-2411-214: To overcome this, we model each sparse point as a minimal anchor unit, representing a localized region characterized by surface geometry, curvature, and point density.
elements.sent-2411-215: A local distribution predictor is introduced to learn adaptive contextual information for each anchor region in a statistically explicit manner.
elements.sent-2411-216: We refer to the combination of the sparser latent subset and its associated local distributions as sparse priors, which are physically encoded and stored.
elements.sent-2411-217: These sparse priors enable more efficient compression and support context-aware binary coding.
elements.sent-2411-218: A key challenge in faithful reconstruction from sparse priors lies in recovering high-resolution latent representations from limited observations.
elements.sent-2411-219: This problem is twofold: (1) generating dense, high-fidelity latent features from minimal sparse inputs, and (2) effectively leveraging local statistical distributions to guide the reconstruction process.
elements.sent-2411-220: To address the first challenge, we adopt diffusion models due to their strong generative capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-221: Diffusion models have demonstrated strong generative capability across both 2D~\cite{wang2025denoising, wang2025uniadapter, que2024denoising, dhariwal2021diffusion and 3D domains~\cite{chen2025discrete, shan2024diffusion, luo2021diffusion , effectively mitigating common issues such as mode collapse and over-smoothing.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Crucially, diffusion enables the synthesis of dense latent representations from pure noise, making it more intuitive to inject sparse priors as conditioning signals rather than attempting to directly upsample sparse inputs to a high-dimensional latent space.
elements.sent-2411-223: For the second challenge, we introduce an attention-based aggregation mechanism that fuses sparse observations with local distribution cues.
elements.sent-2411-224: The resulting distribution parameters are further incorporated into a context-aware entropy model, allowing for efficient compression while maintaining high semantic fidelity in the reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-225: By employing two distinct encoding streams and utilizing the diffusion compression model as an intermediary, our framework separates the latents used for reconstruction from the sparser representations used for storage.
elements.sent-2411-226: This separation enables more flexible size constraints on the latents while ensuring high reconstruction fidelity, even under high compression ratios.
elements.sent-2411-227: We propose a lossy point cloud compression data flow that compresses a point cloud further into sparse priors.
elements.sent-2411-228: We introduce a multi-stage sparsity-guided conditional diffusion scheme.
elements.sent-2411-229: We decouple the sparse priors into inter-point sparse latents and intra-point local distributions, and employ cross-attention to aggregate priors from both scales across varying down-sampling rates.
elements.sent-2411-230: We present a decoupled sparse priors guided diffusion compression model for point cloud, driven by a conditional diffusion model and a context-aware entropy model.

It incorporates a Probabilistic attention-based conditional diffusion model to denoise the latent representations conditioned on the sparse priors.} \label{fig:quickrun}

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, trim=0cm 12.5cm 9cm 1cm]{figs/overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of our decoupled sparse priors guided diffusion compression model for point cloud (DiffCom). Instead of directly encoding the input point cloud into latent points and features, we utilize a sparse point encoder to extract a sparser representations. The sparser representation are decoupled into sparse points and intra-point local distributions. During decompression, we begin with Gaussian noise and apply a Probabilistic attention-based conditional denoiser (PACD) conditioned on the sparse priors, reconstructing the latent representation. elements.sent-2411-260: A common strategy for encoding a point cloud~$X$ is to use a latent point encoder $\Theta_L$, built from a series of Set Abstraction (SA) modules~\cite{he2022density, lyu2021conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-261: These modules progressively downsample the point set using Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) and aggregate neighboring features to a latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-262: This process can be formally expressed as: \begin{equation}(X_l, F_l) = \Theta_L(X)\end{equation} where $X_l=\{x_{l,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_l}\}$ represents the coordinates of the downsampled points and $F_l=\{f_{l,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_l}\}$ their corresponding learned features, which describes the local pattern centered at the downsampled points.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: We call the latent representation $(X_l, F_l)$ as latent point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-264: However, this process introduces significant geometric redundancy as FPS is agnostic to the underlying geometry, for instance, by sampling multiple points from a single continuous surface.
elements.sent-2411-265: This leads to an inefficient representation with highly correlated features, adding unnecessary computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-266: To address this, we propose a dual-data flow, which can recover the latent point cloud with minimal loss based on a more compact representation (sparse priors).
elements.sent-2411-267: We achieve high compression ratio by storing sparse priors instead of latent points.
elements.sent-2411-268: To ensure reconstruction quality, we propose a conditional denoiser that robustly reconstructs the latent point cloud, conditioned on a limited set of sparse priors.
elements.sent-2411-269: Additionally, we propose a context-aware entropy model inspired by derived sparse priors to help binary coding.
elements.sent-2411-270: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:ppl , our proposed DiffCom separately encodes latent representations and sparse priors, augmented by a latent diffusion model guided by the sparse priors.}
elements.sent-2411-271: In the subsequent sections, we provide detailed information on the overall data flow, the conditional denoiser, the entropy model, and the model architecture of our proposed framework.
elements.sent-2411-272: To effectively compress the original point cloud $X$, we address the inherent redundancy within latent points $X_l$ and associated features $F_l$ by introducing a dual-density data flow.
elements.sent-2411-273: Specifically, we aim to use a sparse point encoder to extract a high-level abstraction from the original point cloud $X$.
elements.sent-2411-274: Our sparse point encoder summarizes an input point cloud into sparse points $X_s=\{x_{s,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_s}\}$ , which captures essential skeleton, and associated features $F_s=\{f_{s,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_s}\}$, which capture semantic properties like curvature and regional density within a large receptive field: \begin{equation}(X_s,F_s)=\Theta_S(X)\end{equation} where $N_s\ll{N_l <{N}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-275: The sparse point encoder $\Theta_S$ resembles $\Theta_L$ architecturally, but employs a higher sampling rate.
elements.sent-2411-276: Their training objectives and ultimate goals diverge significantly: The point encoder $\Theta_L$ is trained with point decoder to directly recover the original point cloud, aiming for a stable latent representation capable of high-fidelity reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-277: In contrast, the sparse point encoder is trained jointly with the denoiser, not for direct reconstruction, but to capture the essential skeletons needed to recover a latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-278: The sparse point cloud yields a coarse approximation of the overall shape, akin to fitting a shape template.
elements.sent-2411-279: For simplicity, we will use \(\mathbf{X}\) to denote the pair $(X, F)$ for both sparse and latent point cloud in the remainder of the discussion unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-280: By storing the sparse coded point cloud instead of the latent point cloud, the compression ratio is significantly improved.. Sparse Prior decoupling.
elements.sent-2411-281: The output of our sparse point encoder, the subset $(X_s,F_s)$ , suffers from two main issues: the point locations only coarsely represent the original geometry, and its associated features are ambiguous "black-box" mixtures of semantics.
elements.sent-2411-282: To overcome these limitations, we treat the sparse points as anchors to build a more accurate and meaningful representation.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our goal is to refine the new representation for high-fidelity latent denoising.
elements.sent-2411-284: We choose the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) for our task inspired by its proven success in point cloud registration.
elements.sent-2411-285: In registration, a GMM excels at representing a discrete set of points as a continuous, probabilistic surface.
elements.sent-2411-286: By modeling our sparse points as a GMM, we create a lightweight surrogate that explicitly bridges the information gap without incurring significant storage overhead.
elements.sent-2411-287: In our framework, the point cloud is represented as a probabilistic mixture of anchors: \begin{equation}p(x)=\Sigma_{i=1}^{N_s}w_i(X_s,Y_s)p_i(x\vert \mu_{s_i},\sigma_{s_i})\end{equation} Here GMM is initialized by setting the mean of each Gaussian component to the corresponding sparse point $\mu_{s ^0=X_s$, and the means are then iteratively refined by $\mu_{s_i =X_{s_i +v_i$, the covariance matrix $\sigma_{s_i}$ captures anisotropic properties of local surface, such as the principal directions and degree of stretching.}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm is used to enforce the distribution converge to the real distribution of the input point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-289: Instead of iterative updates, our model learns to execute the clustering and parameter updating in a single forward pass of a lightweight predictor: \begin{equation}(\mu_s,\sigma_s) = \Theta_P(X_s,F_s)\end{equation} The plug-in distribution predictor $\Theta_P$ is implemented as a stack of convolutional layers, yielding a computationally lean and efficient architecture.
elements.sent-2411-290: The likelihood of the entire original point cloud $L=\Pi_{i=1 ^Np(x_i|\mu_s,\sigma_s$) is optimized in the bottleneck, detailed in the loss function section.}
elements.sent-2411-291: The calculation of $w_i$ is also learned during the decoding of latent point cloud and will be detailed in the context model section.
elements.sent-2411-292: The GMM-based representation has two advantages.
elements.sent-2411-293: First, their probabilistic nature directly addresses the ambiguity of the features by modeling the underlying data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-294: Second, by representing the data as a continuous surface, GMM is invariant to point cloud resolutions, allowing us to flexible choose sampling rate for point encoder.
elements.sent-2411-295: Each point, together with its associated features and distribution, forms a highly informative intelligent primitive, which we term sparse priors.
elements.sent-2411-296: These priors integrate both handcrafted and learned cues, providing a rich and decoupled representation of the underlying structure.
elements.sent-2411-297: They are then used as conditioning signals for our PACD model, described in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-298: The resulting predicted latents $\tilde{\mathbf{X _l$ are subsequently passed to a point decoder to reconstruct the final high-quality point cloud $X$.}}

elements.sent-2411-299: Given the inevitable information loss due to the aggressive down-sampling rate, the mapping from sparse points to latent points becomes ill-posed and prone to clustering artifacts in the reconstructed point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-300: To address this, we utilize diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising , renowned for their robust generative capabilities, to encapsulate the inherent variability in the original point cloud $X$, thereby avoiding mode collapse while reconstruction~\cite{luo2021diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-301: The denoiser helps not only enables high-quality reconstruction, but also mitigating inherent outliers through iterative denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-302: To maintain high fidelity of the latents, our DCM incorporates sparse priors into the PACD at each encoding and decoding stage throughout the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-303: The reverse process of diffusion is modeled as a Markov chain, given by: \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{l,0},\cdots,\mathbf{X}_{l,T-1}|\mathbf{X}_{l,T})=\prod_{t=1}^{T}p_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{l,t-1}|\mathbf{X}_{l,t},\mathbf{X}_s).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-304: The stepwise denoising follows a Gaussian distribution: $p_{\theta (\mathbf{X _{l,t-1 |\mathbf{X _{l,t ,\mathbf{X _s)$ and the mean $\mu_{\theta (\mathbf{X _{l,t ,\mathbf{X _s,t)$ is reparameterized as: \begin{equation}\mu_{\theta}(\mathbf{X}_{l,t},\mathbf{X}_s,t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}(\mathbf{X}_{l,t}-\frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}}\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{X}_{l,t},\mathbf{X}_s,t)).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-305: The noise is encapsulated by the denoising network $\epsilon_\theta$, and $\alpha_t$ and $\beta_t$ are predefined small positive constants, and the cumulative product $\bar\alpha_{t}$.
elements.sent-2411-306: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:denoiser , our PACD employs a U-Net-like architecture.}

elements.figure-2411-012: \center{ \includegraphics[trim=0cm 9cm 9.5cm 0cm, clip, width=0.96\linewidth]{figs/encoder.pdf}} \caption{Architecture of the downsampling and upsampling blocks. elements.sent-2411-338: Our approach initiates with an autoencoder $\Theta_L$ that maps the original point cloud $X=\{x_i\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N}\}$ to latent points $X_l=\{x_{l,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^3 |1\leq{i}\leq{N_L}\}$ and corresponding features $F_{l =\{f_{l,i}\in{\mathbb{R ^{d |1\leq{i}\leq{N_L}\}$: \begin{equation}\Theta_L: X\in{\mathbb{R}^3}\mapsto(X_l, F_l).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-339: After training, we discard the encoder and keep only the decoder for decompression.
elements.sent-2411-340: In the compression scenario, guidance information is unavailable during decompression, and the use of skip connections between the encoder and decoder is also prohibited (since we do not have the full data during decompression).
elements.sent-2411-341: Therefore, we follow the autoencoder backbone in D-PCC~\cite{he2022density consisting of downsampling and upsampling stages.}
elements.sent-2411-342: Each encoding stage begins by downsampling the point cloud with a predefined downsampling rate using the farthest point sampling algorithm to ensure maximum coverage.
elements.sent-2411-343: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:encoder (a), each downsample block integrates explicit local encoding and implicit feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Initially, the point cloud is mapped to a downsampled version $P_{s+1}$, aggregating the neighbouring information into features $F_{s+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-345: Let us denote the retained key points as $p$ and its neighbouring points in the original point cloud as $C(p)$.
elements.sent-2411-346: For each $p_k\in{C(p)}$, compute the direction from $p$ to $p_k$, and their Euclidean distance, which jointly characterize the local surface variations.. where $p\in{P _{s+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-347: The geometric variations are further refined via self-gating.
elements.sent-2411-348: Additionally, a transformer aggregates semantic features from stage $s$ by treating $P_{s+1}$ as the query, $P_s$ as the key, and $F_s$ as the value.
elements.sent-2411-349: Finally, an MLP merges both explicit position embedding and implicit ancestor features from stage $s$.. Point Decoder.
elements.sent-2411-350: Symmetrically, in the decoding stage $s$, the goal is to upsample points from $(P_s, F_s)$.
elements.sent-2411-351: The upsample block is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:encoder (b).}
elements.sent-2411-352: Specifically, during the upsampling of $P_{s}$ to generate $\tilde{P _{s+1}$, a pre-defined number of candidates are defined.}
elements.sent-2411-353: To mitigate artifacts in $\tilde{F _{s+1}$, we also apply a sub-point convolution technique as D-PCC~\cite{he2022density .}}
elements.sent-2411-354: For each candidate, direction and scale are predicted from $F_s$, and the weighted sum is computed as the residual for $\tilde{C (p)$.}
elements.sent-2411-355: The Sub-Point Convolution will generate $k>f_s$ upsampled point candidates, from each we select the top $f_s$ points and associated features.
elements.sent-2411-356: Unlike D-PCC, our method uses $f_s$ to control the number of expanded points, rather than a learnable $\tilde{C (p)$.}
elements.sent-2411-357: This is motivated by our empirical observation that the learned upsampling rate consistently converges to the fixed downsampling rate in the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-358: Following the $s$ upsampling stages, we apply an upsampling module with a rate of 1 for final refinement.. Set Abstraction Module.
elements.sent-2411-359: Set abstraction (SA) modules~\cite{lyu2023controllable are utilized to carry out downsampling in the denoiser.}
elements.sent-2411-360: For simplicity, we denote the concatenated input $(\tilde{X _l^k, X_s^k)$ as $X_k=\{x_j|1\leq{j}\leq{N_k}\}$, where $x_j$ denotes the 3D coordinates and $N_k$ is the number of points at level $k$, the associated features are given by $F_k=\{f_j|1\leq{j}\leq{N_k}\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-361: The concatenation $(X_k, F_k)$ is of size $N_k \times (d_k + 3)$.
elements.sent-2411-362: The module starts by using iterative Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) to reduce the number of points to $N_{k+1}$: $\{x_j|1\leq{j}\leq{N_{k+1 }\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-363: For each sampled point $x_j$, it identifies $K$ nearest neighbors from the input of the last layer $(X_k, F_k)$.
elements.sent-2411-364: The neighbors' coordinates and features are extracted as matrix of shape $N_{k+1 \times K \times (d_k + 3)$, which is processed by a shared multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to produce a new matrix with dimensions $N_{k+1 \times K \times d_{k+1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-365: A self-attention layer is then applied to aggregate the features from $K$ neighbors, yielding the output matrix $F_{k+1}$ of size $N_{k+1 \times d_{k+1}$.. Feature Propagation Module.}
elements.sent-2411-366: Feature propagation (FP) modules~\cite{lyu2023controllable are utilized to carry out downsampling in the denoiser.}
elements.sent-2411-367: In the feature propagation module, each FP layer is the reverse process of the corresponding SA layer: from \(\{x_j|1\leq{j}\leq{N_{k+1 }\}\) to \(\{x_j|1\leq{j}\leq{N_k}\}\).}
elements.sent-2411-368: This is achieved by finding the neighbors \(\{y_n|{n}\in{B_y(x_j)}\} \subseteq \{y_n|1\leq{n}\leq{N_{k+1 }\}\) for each \(x_j\).}
elements.sent-2411-369: The features at these neighbors are transformed via a shared MLP and aggregated to \(x_j\) through an attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-370: Finally, the upsampled features are concatenated with the skip-connected features from the corresponding SA module and processed through a naive PointNet~\cite{qiPointNetDeepLearning2017 .. Sparse Point Encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-371: To generate homogeneous conditions for our HMA-guided latent diffusion model, we adopt the same architecture as the point encoder, but with a higher down-sample rate to extract a sparser point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-372: Firstly, we pre-train the encoder $\Theta_L$ and decoder $\Omega_L$ using the Chamfer Distance (CD) loss only.
elements.sent-2411-373: Then we train the diffusion model to learn the noise $\epsilon$, and the reconstruction loss is given by: \begin{small \begin{equation}\ell_{recon}=\mathcal{E}_{(X_l,X_s)\sim{P_{data}}}\|\epsilon-\epsilon_{\theta}(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha_t}})X_l+\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha_t}}\epsilon, X_l,X_s)\|^2\end{equation} \end{small For compression loss $\ell_{comp}$, the Bits Per Point (BPP) is approximated by the likelihood of sparse points and distributions: \begin{equation}\ell_{comp}=E_{\hat{Y}_s}[-\log_2(p_{\hat{Y}_s}(\hat{Y}_s)] + E_{\hat{Z}_s}[-\log_2(p_{\hat{Z}_s}(\hat{Z}_s)]\end{equation} The final loss of denoiser is a weighted sum of two losses.}}

elements.sent-2411-414: In Variant 1 (M$_1$), we remove the denoiser and directly encode the latent points, reducing their size to match that of the sparse points.
elements.sent-2411-415: Since it contains only the autoencoder, we refer to this as a 1-stage model.
elements.sent-2411-416: In Variant 2 (M$_2$), we use only sparse points as priors without predicting their distribution.
elements.sent-2411-417: In Variant 3, we predict the distribution as additional sparse priors but do not incorporate them into the entropy model.
elements.sent-2411-419: Comparing M$_2$, M$_3$, M$_4$ with M$_1$, decoupling sparse priors results in around 64\% savings in bitrates and 24\% reduction in Chamfer Distance, mainly because the 1-stage model struggles to decrease the lower bound of entropy with latent points constrained by reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-420: Comparing M$_3$, M$_4$ with M$_2$, the distributions provide valuable guidance for both latent denoising and bitrate reduction during the binary coding process, with only a marginal increase ($\approx$10\%) in bits.
elements.sent-2411-421: The effectiveness of these distributions in the context-aware entropy model is further analyzed in Table~\ref{tab:abl_entropy and in the subsequent paragraph.}

elements.sent-2411-431: Table~\ref{tab:abl_entropy highlights the benefits of including PACD and distributions in the binary coding of sparse points in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-432: In the first and third groups of the table, predicting distribution through convolutional layers outperforms through MLP by 0.13, 0.03 in BPP and 1.2, 0.3 in Chamfer Distance, which can be linked to the larger receptive field, enabling more accurate prediction of the distributions.
elements.sent-2411-433: Although point clouds are unordered, we hypothesize that the order of points influences the contextual distribution during the binary sequential point-to-point coding process.
elements.sent-2411-434: In the second group, we demonstrate that keeping the dimension of local distribution same as sparse points improves both metrics, as more detailed inter-point dependencies help identify critical key points from higher levels.
elements.sent-2411-435: Furthermore, the second and third groups demonstrate the advantages of using a context-aware entropy model for feature coding rather than point coding, as points are inherently sparse and require fewer bits, which is less than the additional storage introduced by the distributions.
elements.sent-2411-436: Conversely, feature coding balances the bit reduction with the cost of storing the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-437: The last group emphasizes the efficiency of local distribution in binary coding and PACD.
elements.sent-2411-438: By maintaining a consistent distribution size alongside the sparse points, binary coding efficiency is enhanced, leading to a lower bits-per-point metric.
elements.sent-2411-439: We propose a decoupled sparse priors-guided compression framework, DiffCom, which addresses the inherent redundancy in latent representations by separating latent points, used for high-quality reconstruction, from sparse priors, designed for efficient storage and transmission.
elements.sent-2411-440: The sparse priors are further decoupled using a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) into sparse points and local distributions for accurate and explainable geometry coding.
elements.sent-2411-441: DiffCom employs a probabilistic attention-based conditional denoiser (PACD) to reconstruct latent points conditioned on the sparse priors, enabling high-quality reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-442: Furthermore, it leverages a context-aware entropy model for efficient binary coding, minimizing data loss while preserving both geometric and semantic fidelity.
